chapters.title: When language used to flow on paper
chapters.text:
Speeches used to come after long, anxious preparations. Writing used to mean books, newspapers, magazines, and academic articles. Writing used to be the most polished and perfect expression of ideas. Those days are gone. Edited and formal writing is still very much alive, but unedited and unfiltered words, the kind that used to belong only to speech, now sit at the top of the charts. Writing has become more conversational and informal. Everyone writes today. With the boundless creativity of Internet language, editors are no longer a requirement. The age of long writing processes is ending.

Internet acronyms like IDK, which means I do not know, cut down on the number of letters you type when chatting with someone.

The digital turn has changed writing from something that intimidated people facing a blank page into something anyone can use to express themselves with ease. It is the golden age of social acronyms, slang, memes, emoji, and the rest of Internet communication culture. Whatever register you grew up with, this evolution of language is a welcome shift in the writing system.

In this summary you will meet the forces that shape our language and affect how we connect with others. You will also see the parallels between emojis and physical gestures. You will never look at your online chats the same way again.

Gretchen McCulloch has written that when she sees the boundless creativity of Internet language flowing past her online, she cannot help but want to understand how it works.

Gretchen McCulloch.

chapters.title: Your peers shape how you speak
chapters.text:
Every generation has its own language habits. Social factors like where you live, your parents, your school, and your friends shape how you communicate. Most children grow up using the speech patterns of their parents, because every family speaks its own dialect. These dialects include misheard song lyrics, onomatopoeia, and words children invent on their own. Our language mix widens as our world widens. At school we meet social styles that blend with what we learned at home. Teenage years are the stage where the strongest language influence shifts from parents to peers. Most young people have access to social media by then, which is a powerful platform for linguistic influence. That is one way children pick up swearing, despite the heroic efforts of most parents to keep cuss words out of their kids' mouths.

Our peer groups are the reason every generation has used words that sound different from their parents' generation.

Social peers drive most language change. Without social influence, we would all still be speaking like Shakespeare. It is also worth noting that we all make language choices, since we can choose to sound British, American, or Canadian. Sometimes we line up with less powerful groups to show belonging and solidarity. None of this happens in a vacuum. We pick up cues from the people around us and from the powers that be. Teens blend formal and informal styles in writing, and it is clearly not from laziness or a failed attempt at formal speech. Internet writing is its own genre of communication with its own goals.

chapters.title: Five kinds of Internet people
chapters.text:
The Internet holds more users than any single country, so our online language should fit social purposes. Internet users have carved out a place in history in five distinct ways.

Old Internet People are the first wave of people to go online. They know the early Internet and can relate to old-timers and hand-coded HTML websites. They are the founding generation who talked to strangers online before most of their friends had networked computers. Old Internet People did not find the Internet easy. You had to be skilled in coding and programming to run even the simplest tasks.

Full Internet People are the second wave. They came online as the Internet went mainstream in the late 1990s and early 2000s. Unlike the first wave, they started out by communicating mostly with people they already knew. They became the first Facebook and Twitter users when those networks launched a few years after they joined the social web. Full Internet People barely remember an Internet without social media slang and are very comfortable with emoji.

Semi-Internet People are also part of the second wave, but their relationship with the network is different because they came to the Internet for professional reasons. They started with work and only later moved on to shopping, reading the news, and planning travel. Semi-Internet People began with email, not Facebook.

Pre-Internet People joined the Internet more slowly, because they could not avoid it forever. Pre-Internet People do not rely on Internet acronyms. When they want to separate thoughts, they use commas, hyphens, or strings of periods.

Post-Internet People were born right in the middle of the Internet's rise. They have been shaped by the Internet socially, even if their own level of use varies. This group tackled the broader problem of context collapse by inventing more lively and versatile ways to interact online.

chapters.title: Typing the tone of your voice
chapters.text:
Some language rules on the Internet depend on a typed-in tone of voice. A clear example is the use of all caps to signal emphasis, strong feeling, or even shouting. Emphatic caps may look like an Internet quirk, but the practice goes back well before the web. A message written in all caps, like I LOVE YOU, tells the reader that the tone is happy or even happier than usual.

Emphatic caps are a clean way to express strong feeling and have survived the move from letter writing to the Internet era.

Repeating letters is another old informal writing tool that works especially well for emotional words like yay or no. Another way to show tone is the sarcasm tilde. The tilde is a punctuation mark that signals sarcasm in the absence of physical cues like a raised eyebrow or a wry smile. When other punctuation marks fall short, the tilde keeps sarcasm subtle and context-aware online.

Periods at the end of utterances, lengthened letters, all caps, irony punctuation, minimal punctuation, and capital letters paired with line breaks all belong to the system of typed tone, and the system works. Still, a need arose to express thoughts with more accuracy, and that is where emoji stepped in.

Gretchen McCulloch has noted that repeating letters is another way to express emphasis online.

chapters.title: Why emoji and gifs filled a real gap
chapters.text:
To some extent, punctuation does a solid job of showing tone of voice, but there is still a need for something more embodied. Our bodies are part of how we communicate. We live in a world where a friend could look you in the eye, grin, and say you are the most terrible person I have ever met. So you can see the need for a kind of body-and-face representation in chat. To fill that gap, emojis and emoticons were introduced. Emoticons are the smiley faces made from punctuation marks, letters, and numbers. Emojis are the small pictures of faces, objects, and symbols. Emoticons have faded on the web, while emojis are still going strong. With an emoji, you tell people that you hear and understand what they are saying. Emojis are a form of gestural emblem and have become an essential and fun part of online communication.

Emojis help communicate emotions in ways that words cannot capture.

There is a wide range of emojis on different platforms to send simple gestures such as a thumbs up, a wave, a smile, an eye roll, and even a wink. Some carry more suggestive ideas, like the eggplant emoji. Emojis still thrive because they are not tied to a specific language. Animated gifs are another well-known way to express gestures online. Technically, gifs are looping silent image files that can show any image. They are image files of gestures acted out by humans, cartoon characters, and even animals. Gifs are a lot of fun and a clear trend on the Internet today. The most widely used emojis are the face with tears of joy and the loudly crying face.

According to the British Broadcasting Corporation, Japan was the first country to roll out emoji on mobile phones in the 1990s.

chapters.title: How conversation keeps changing
chapters.text:
For an older person, the word hello still carries a hint of calling someone over, while a younger person hears it as a simple greeting. The same shift happened with hey and hi, which used to feel like a summoning sound and now feel like friendly greetings. That small change in hello, hey, and hi is only one example of how greetings have shifted.

The word dear in emails once sounded oddly intimate, but as it spread through business emails, people had to lean into it.

People are getting used to shorter greetings in both formal and informal settings. The effect shows up beyond greetings and shapes how we communicate more broadly. Before the Internet, landline phone calls were the norm, but now people chat more than they call. We even schedule video calls ahead of time.

As the Internet grew, people poured more energy into social media. Many discovered platforms that let them build and keep friendships online. The Internet went mainstream, and people, especially teens, found a third place in it, somewhere apart from home and work. Status messages and tweets became part of the daily fabric. Then we moved into mobile-first platforms like Snapchat and Instagram, where you can post images and short videos. We were even introduced to short stories that vanish after twenty-four hours. Language lives in people's minds at a particular moment, so you cannot be too rigid about it. The communication habits of the newer generation may look strange to older generations, but it helps to remember that there is always a reason people choose to speak the way they do.

chapters.title: Memes and the wider Internet metaphor
chapters.text:
Richard Dawkins coined the word meme in 1976 as an ideological counterpart to genes. His work was later borrowed and refined for use online. The idea of the meme arrived at a moment when people were just getting used to the Internet.

Beyond words, images, and videos going viral, memes are really about remaking and recombining online communication.

Memes have been part of technology for a long time and are stitched into the Internet today. You might think memes only appeared recently as part of online changes, but the culture goes back well before the Internet. What we have today is the product of long technical adjustments. Any community that talks online now has its own set of memes. There are memes on almost every topic, from video games to anime to parenting, and there are even memes on politics. It is still unclear exactly why memes pull us in, or how we naturally latch onto the in-jokes.

A single book is not the only home of a language. Even dictionaries are not the source of the words we use. They are records of languages already in use, so it is wrong to see them as the origin. So we can let go of the book metaphor. Language is more than a book. It is not static or authoritative, and it is not just a messy word pile that needs organizing. Language is infinite, and no book can hold every word in use. When all we know is formal language, our vocabulary runs thin. Informal language opens our minds to more possibilities.

There are about seven thousand languages in the world, and most of them have little or no presence on the Internet. The Internet should welcome every language. Treating language as the Internet rather than a book makes room for creativity, innovation, and linguistic play.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The Internet has been a major influence on communication, and a welcome one. Old cultural habits have changed and new ones have arrived. Things keep shifting. The energy behind collective social relationships through tech-driven communication keeps growing. We wait for technical changes that bring more languages online, just as we wait for the gap between generations to close. Soon, there will be no generation left who has not been touched by the Internet. The Internet has become real life, and there is room on this large linguistic web for everyone.

Learn to use the Internet to communicate better, because it is a rich source of information and makes daily life easier. It is also a free source of entertainment, including online games, browsing, music, chat, and drama. The Internet has also made it easy to find our way with GPS. Still, there are downsides. It can be a time sink, and some parents see it as unsafe for their children. Online shopping and credit card use have brought new kinds of fraud. Do not let all of your social media time be about entertainment. Set aside time to learn new things and build meaningful networks.

Try this. Add a bit of creativity to your Internet life by using emoji and memes. Use them to express yourself so the person you are chatting with fully understands what you mean.
